Why I Bought a 2015 MacBook Air in 2026

Sun, Jun 7, 2026

Buying a laptop from 2015 in 2026 sounds backwards at first.

On paper, it is easy to argue against it. The machine is old. The specs are modest. It does not look impressive next to modern ultrabooks. But I was not trying to buy the most impressive machine. I was trying to buy the right tool for the way I actually work.

Why this specific model

I wanted a laptop that feels focused.

The 2015 MacBook Air has a few things I still value a lot:

  • A reliable keyboard with full travel
  • Legacy ports that reduce adapter chaos
  • A lightweight body that is easy to carry
  • Enough battery life for writing and research sessions

It is not a machine for heavy video work or large local AI workloads, and I did not buy it for that. I bought it for writing, editing text, browsing documentation, handling email standards testing, and maintaining small websites.

Price-to-purpose made sense

The price was a big part of the decision.

I could buy a used 2015 Air for a fraction of the cost of a new laptop and still cover most of my daily tasks. For my workload, the value was hard to ignore. I would rather spend the difference on backups, domain renewals, and tools that directly improve my workflow.

Fewer distractions, better sessions

There is another reason I did not expect to appreciate this much: constraints.

Because this machine is not overpowered, I naturally avoid the "open everything, run everything" habit. I work in clearer blocks. Fewer apps at once, fewer background tabs, and less context switching. That has made my writing sessions calmer and more consistent.

What I changed in my setup

I adjusted my workflow to match the hardware:

  • I keep browser tabs intentionally low
  • I prefer static-site workflows for content projects
  • I avoid dependency-heavy experiments on this machine
  • I reserve heavier tasks for a newer computer when needed

That split works surprisingly well. The Air handles the core work. More demanding tasks happen elsewhere.

The honest trade-offs

This is not a "perfect vintage laptop" story.

There are trade-offs:

  • Performance ceilings are real
  • Modern web apps can feel heavy
  • Battery health depends entirely on the unit you buy
  • You need to accept that some workflows belong on newer hardware

None of this bothered me once I defined what this laptop is for.

Final thought

I bought a 2015 MacBook Air in 2026 because I wanted a dependable writing-and-web machine with less noise around the work.

If your workflow is mostly text, lightweight development, documentation, and publishing, older hardware can still be a smart choice. Not because nostalgia is trendy, but because a clear purpose beats raw specs almost every time.
